verdict_reasoning: STIM1 is a single-pass type-1 transmembrane protein that resides predominantly in the ER membrane, where it senses luminal Ca2+ depletion via its EF-hand domain. Upon store depletion, STIM1 oligomerizes and translocates to ER-PM junctions where it clusters and gates Orai1 channels at the plasma membrane surface. During this activation state, the C-terminal cytoplasmic domain faces the cytosol and clusters near the PM, but a small fraction of STIM1 has also been documented at the plasma membrane itself (with an extracellular N-terminal EF-hand domain accessible from outside). This constitutes a documented minority PM pool accessible from the extracellular face, dependent on activation/store-depletion state. Thus surface accessibility is real but context-dependent (ER-PM junctional clustering during signaling), fitting dual_localization/cell_state_induced contextual biology rather than constitutive surface expression.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Extent of stable constitutive PM surface pool vs. activation-induced ER-PM junctional clustering; some reports document PM-resident STIM1 even at baseline in certain cell types.
